title: match Statements
summary: Learn how Python's match statement lets you compare a value against multiple patterns cleanly and expressively.

blocks.md:
Imagine you work at a coffee shop and someone orders a drink. You look at the order slip and route it to the right station: espresso goes here, tea goes there, smoothie goes somewhere else. You're **matching** the order to an action.

Python's `match` statement does exactly that — it looks at a value and finds the pattern that fits, then runs the code for that pattern. It's a cleaner, more readable alternative to writing a long chain of `if/elif/else` checks.

blocks.title: Python 3.10+ required
blocks.md: `match` was introduced in **Python 3.10**. If you're on an older version, you'll get a `SyntaxError`. Run `python --version` to check. Most modern systems have 3.10 or later.
The basic structure looks like this: you write `match` followed by the value you want to examine, then a series of `case` branches — one for each pattern you want to handle.

blocks.code:
day = "Monday"

match day:
    case "Monday":
        print("Start of the work week!")
    case "Friday":
        print("Almost the weekend!")
    case "Saturday" | "Sunday":
        print("It's the weekend!")
    case _:
        print("Just another weekday.")
blocks.caption: match checks day against each case in order and runs the first match.

blocks.md:
Notice a few things:
- Each `case` is followed by a **pattern** (like `"Monday"`) and a colon.
- Python checks patterns **top to bottom** and stops at the first match.
- The `_` at the end is the **wildcard** — it matches anything that didn't match above. Think of it as the `else` of a `match` statement.

blocks.title: The _ is your safety net
blocks.md: Think of `case _:` like the last line in a restaurant menu that says "Chef's Choice" — it's what you get when nothing else on the menu matched your request. It guarantees the code always has something to do.

blocks.code:
status_code = 404

match status_code:
    case 200:
        print("OK — everything is fine.")
    case 404:
        print("Not Found — the page doesn't exist.")
    case 500:
        print("Server Error — something broke.")
    case _:
        print(f"Unknown status: {status_code}")
blocks.output: Not Found — the page doesn't exist.
blocks.caption: Matching integers works just like matching strings.

blocks.md: Sometimes several different values should trigger the same action. You can combine them in one `case` using the pipe `|` symbol, which means **"or"**.

blocks.code:
grade = "B"

match grade:
    case "A":
        print("Excellent!")
    case "B" | "C":
        print("Good work, keep it up!")
    case "D" | "F":
        print("Let's study together and try again.")
    case _:
        print("I don't recognise that grade.")
blocks.caption: Use | to group values that share the same outcome.

blocks.md: You can do everything `match` does with `if/elif/else`. So why use `match`? Because when you have many possible values, `match` is **easier to read and harder to get wrong**. Here's the same logic side by side:

blocks.caption: The if/elif version works, but notice how repetitive day == is.

blocks.code:
# Using match — cleaner and more expressive
day = "Saturday"

match day:
    case "Monday":
        print("Start of the work week!")
    case "Friday":
        print("Almost the weekend!")
    case "Saturday" | "Sunday":
        print("It's the weekend!")
    case _:
        print("Just another weekday.")
blocks.caption: The match version removes repetition and reads almost like plain English.

blocks.md: `match` gets really powerful when your value has **structure** — like a tuple (a pair of values). You can match on the shape and the contents at the same time.

blocks.code:
point = (0, 5)

match point:
    case (0, 0):
        print("Origin — the center point.")
    case (x, 0):
        print(f"On the X-axis at {x}.")
    case (0, y):
        print(f"On the Y-axis at {y}.")
    case (x, y):
        print(f"Somewhere at ({x}, {y}).")
blocks.caption: match can destructure a tuple — x and y capture the actual values.

blocks.md: In that example, `(0, y)` is a pattern that means: "a tuple whose first item is `0`, and whose second item I'll call `y`". Python fills in `y` automatically. This is called **destructuring** and it's one of the most elegant parts of `match`.

blocks.title: Variables in patterns capture, they don't compare
blocks.md:
When you write `case x:` (a plain name with no quotes), Python **captures** the value into `x` — it does NOT check if the value equals some existing variable named `x`. Only **literals** (strings in quotes, numbers) and the wildcard `_` are used for comparison.

```python
x = 10
match 5:
    case x:   # This always matches! It captures 5 into x, overwriting it.
        print(x)  # prints 5, not 10
```

If you want to compare against an existing variable, use an `if` guard or a different approach.

blocks.title: No break needed
blocks.md: Unlike `switch` in some other languages (like JavaScript or C), Python's `match` does **not** fall through from one case to the next. Once a case matches and its code runs, `match` is done. You never need a `break` statement.

blocks.question: What does the _ pattern in a case branch mean?
options: It matches only the value None.
It matches anything that wasn't matched by a previous case.
It raises an error if reached.
It skips the match statement entirely.
blocks.explanation: The _ wildcard is the catch-all pattern. It matches any value, so placing it last makes it the default branch — exactly like else at the end of an if/elif chain.

keyTakeaways: `match` compares a value against a list of `case` patterns and runs the first match — cleaner than long if/elif chains.
The `_` wildcard acts as the default catch-all case and should come last.
Use `|` inside a `case` to match multiple values with the same action.
`match` can destructure tuples and sequences, capturing parts of the value into variables.
`match` requires Python 3.10 or newer — check your version before using it.

exercise.prompt:
Write a function called `describe_season(month)` that accepts a month number (1–12) and uses a `match` statement to return a string describing the season. Use these groupings:
- December, January, February → "Winter"
- March, April, May → "Spring"
- June, July, August → "Summer"
- September, October, November → "Autumn"
- Any other number → "Unknown month"

Test it by printing the result for months 3, 7, 11, and 99.
exercise.starterCode:
def describe_season(month):
    match month:
        # add your cases here
        pass

print(describe_season(3))
print(describe_season(7))
print(describe_season(11))
print(describe_season(99))
exercise.hint: Each case should list the month numbers separated by |. For example: `case 12 | 1 | 2:` covers winter months. Return a string from each branch.
exercise.solution:
def describe_season(month):
    match month:
        case 12 | 1 | 2:
            return "Winter"
        case 3 | 4 | 5:
            return "Spring"
        case 6 | 7 | 8:
            return "Summer"
        case 9 | 10 | 11:
            return "Autumn"
        case _:
            return "Unknown month"

print(describe_season(3))   # Spring
print(describe_season(7))   # Summer
print(describe_season(11))  # Autumn
print(describe_season(99))  # Unknown month
